title: Cranberry sauce
subtitle: Tart, just-set, made in 15 minutes
excerpt: Fresh or frozen cranberries cooked with orange juice and sugar until they burst and the sauce sets. Ready in 15 minutes and better than anything from a jar. Served at room temperature or cold alongside turkey, gammon, and cold Christmas meats. The set should be loose and spoonable, not stiff like jam.
sourceNotes: Cranberry sauce with turkey is a tradition borrowed from North America that became standard in British Christmas cooking during the 20th century. The British version is typically less sweet than the American one and is often made with orange rather than just water. Fresh cranberries are available from October; frozen ones work equally well and are available year-round.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to 3 months.
recipe.batchNotes: Make a large batch and jar it. Keeps in the fridge for 2 weeks.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to 2 weeks ahead and refrigerate. The flavour improves after a day.

content.text: Cranberries are high in pectin and will set naturally without added pectin as they cool. The sauce will look runnier in the pan than it will be at room temperature. Do not reduce it to the point of stiffness — it will continue to thicken significantly as it cools.

Put the {{cranberries}}, {{granulated-sugar}}, and the juice and zest of the {{orange}} in a medium saucepan. Add 50 ml water. Cook over medium heat, stirring occasionally, for 10 to 15 minutes. The cranberries will burst, the liquid will thicken, and the sauce will turn a deep red. It should look slightly loose at this stage. Taste and add more sugar if needed. Transfer to a bowl or jar and leave to cool. The sauce will set to a spoonable consistency as it reaches room temperature. Serve at room temperature or cold.

items.symptom: Sauce too stiff and jelly-like when cold
items.fix: Stir in a tablespoon of orange juice or water to loosen. The high pectin content of cranberries means the sauce sets quickly — take it off the heat while it still looks pourable

items.symptom: Sauce too tart even after adding sugar
items.cause: Cranberries vary in acidity; some batches are more sour
items.fix: Add sugar in tablespoon increments, tasting between additions. A pinch of fine salt counteracts bitterness and can help balance acidity

items.cause: Sugar added to a cold pan or stirred in after cooling
items.fix: Add the sugar at the start with the cranberries and liquid, before any heat. The juice from the bursting cranberries dissolves it naturally

content.text: Cranberry sauce is the Christmas condiment that pairs with turkey, gammon, and cold meats through Christmas and Boxing Day. It is also used in sandwiches made with leftover turkey the following day. The homemade version takes 15 minutes and keeps in the fridge for two weeks, which makes the jarred version difficult to justify.
